1: Ps 81: Asaph's Festival Psalm — 'Gittith.' 'Gittith' (al hagittit) is a musical term of uncertain origin: possibly connected to Gath (a Philistine lute), to the wine-press (gat = wine vat, suggesting an autumn harvest festival), or to a place or melody name. The psalm divides into two parts: a joyful call to festival worship (vv1-5b) and a divine oracle reviewing the covenant history and lamenting Israel's unfaithfulness (vv5c-16). The transition from human voice to divine voice (v5c: 'I heard a voice I had not known') is one of the most dramatic shifts in the Psalter. The psalm may have been used at the Festival of Tabernacles (Sukkot) or at the New Year (Rosh Hashanah), when the trumpet blast of v3 would have been liturgically fitting.
2: 'Raise a song; sound the tambourine, the sweet lyre with the harp' — the festival instrumentation: toph (tambourine/hand drum, associated with Miriam's celebration at the sea, Exod 15:20), kinnor naim (sweet/beautiful lyre), and nevel (harp). The plurality of instruments signals a full temple orchestra. 'Raise a song' (seu zimrah) — the imperative of festive praise: the community is summoned to collective musical performance.
3: 'Blow the trumpet at the new moon, at the full moon, on our feast day' — tiqeu vachodesh shofar (blow the ram's horn at the new moon). The shofar (ram's horn) was blown at new moons (Num 10:10) and at the Feast of Trumpets (Rosh Hashanah, the seventh month's new moon: Lev 23:24; Num 29:1). The 'full moon' (bakeseh, at the covering/full moon) may refer to Passover (first month, 15th day — full moon) or Tabernacles (seventh month, 15th day — also full moon). The dual reference could encompass both spring and fall festivals, or specifically Tabernacles (Sukkot) when the moon is full and the harvest complete.
4: 'For it is a statute for Israel, a rule of the God of Jacob' — ki choq leYisrael hu mishpat le-Elohei Yaakov — the festival is not voluntary but covenantally mandated: a choq (statute, the engraved/fixed law) and a mishpat (ordinance, the legal ruling). The festival calendar was a covenant obligation, not a cultural tradition. YHWH's claim over time — over the calendar — is an expression of his sovereignty over the whole of life.
5: 'He made it a decree in Joseph when he went out over the land of Egypt. I heard a language I had not known' — the transition verse. 'Over the land of Egypt' — the Passover statute was established when YHWH 'went out' over Egypt (Exod 12:12-13: 'I will pass through the land of Egypt that night'). Then: 'I heard a language I had not known' (sephath lo-yadati eshma) — the psalm's voice suddenly shifts: someone hears an unfamiliar language. Most commentators take this as the beginning of YHWH's divine oracle, where YHWH speaks in first person for the remainder of the psalm. The 'unfamiliar language' may be the divine speech itself — the experience of receiving the word of YHWH.
6: 'I relieved your shoulder of the burden; your hands were freed from the basket' — YHWH speaks in first person, recounting the Exodus. 'The burden on the shoulder' (misevel shichmo) and 'the basket' (dudim) — the specific labor of Egyptian slavery: carrying heavy loads on the shoulders and working clay in brick-making baskets. Exod 1:11-14 describes exactly this labor. The physical liberation from literal burden is the Exodus in miniature.
7: 'In distress you called, and I delivered you; I answered you in the secret place of thunder; I tested you at the waters of Meribah' — the divine first-person recital of the Exodus events: (1) the cry from Egypt (ba-tzarah qarata, you called in distress) and the divine response (Exod 3:7-9: YHWH heard the cry); (2) the divine response 'in the secret place of thunder' (beseter raam) — the Sinai theophany of Exod 19-20; (3) the testing at Meribah (Exod 17:1-7; Num 20:1-13) — the water-from-rock incident where Israel tested YHWH.
8: 'Hear, O my people, while I admonish you! O Israel, if you would but listen to me!' — the divine appeal (shema ammi vaaodicha, hear my people and I will warn/testify you) — the covenant lawsuit tone: YHWH as both the rescuing covenant partner and the plaintiff who demands a hearing. 'If you would listen' (im-tishma li) — the conditional formulation: the covenant blessings are contingent on listening. This is the Deuteronomic refrain: Deut 6:3-4; 11:13-14; the Shema itself is the listening command.
9: 'There shall be no strange god among you; you shall not bow down to a foreign god' — the First Commandment restated (Exod 20:3): 'no other gods before me.' 'Strange god' (el zar) and 'foreign god' (el nekar) — both terms for the rival deities of surrounding nations. The prohibition is covenantal: YHWH has an exclusive claim on Israel's allegiance. The covenant violation being diagnosed in the divine speech is precisely this idolatry.
10: 'I am the LORD your God, who brought you up out of the land of Egypt. Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it' — the Decalogue self-identification (Exod 20:2: 'I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt') followed by a remarkable promise: 'Open your mouth wide, and I will fill it' (harkhev picha vaemale'ehu). The open mouth as the posture of requesting: the covenant God offers unlimited provision to the one who asks with full expectancy. The metaphor may be from the feeding of young birds (Ps 147:9) or from the royal court where the king fills the open mouths of petitioners.
11: 'But my people did not listen to my voice; Israel would not submit to me' — the divine lament: lo-shama ammi leqoli (my people did not listen to my voice). The contrast with v8's appeal ('if you would listen') and v10's promise is stark: the invitation was refused. 'Would not submit' (lo-ava, Qal of avah, to be willing, to consent) — Israel refused to consent to YHWH's sovereignty. The ANE treaty violation: the vassal refuses the suzerain's terms.
12: 'So I gave them over to their stubborn hearts, to follow their own counsels' — ashalcheihu bishrerut libbam (I sent them away in the stubbornness of their heart). 'Gave them over' — the divine abandonment to consequence: YHWH does not destroy but withdraws, leaving Israel to the path it chose. Paul uses identical logic in Rom 1:24-28 (God 'gave them over'). The 'stubborn heart' (sherirut lev) — the Jeremiah vocabulary for Israel's unreformed heart (Jer 3:17; 7:24; 9:14; 11:8; 13:10; 18:12; 23:17).
13: 'Oh, that my people would listen to me, that Israel would walk in my ways!' — lu ammi shomea li (if only my people would listen to me) — the divine yearning in the 'if only' conditional (lu). This is the closest YHWH comes to lament: a mourning over unrealized possibility. The conditional shows that YHWH does not coerce: the doors of covenant blessing are open, contingent on Israel's listening. The divine grief over refused grace anticipates Isa 65:2 ('I held out my hands all day to a rebellious people') and Matt 23:37 ('How often I would have gathered your children...').
14: 'I would soon subdue their enemies and turn my hand against their foes' — the conditional promise: had Israel listened, YHWH would have defeated the enemies. The 'quickly' (kimat) of divine victory contrasts with the prolonged suffering of disobedience. The ease of divine military victory makes the current distress all the more tragic: the battles that Israel has fought and lost, YHWH would have won 'quickly.'
15: 'Those who hate the LORD would cringe before him, and their fate would last forever' — 'those who hate the LORD' (mesane YHWH) would become false flatterers (yekhachashelu lo, would dissemble/cringe before him) — the enemy's posture of enforced submission. 'Their fate would last forever' (veyihu itam leolam) — the submission would be permanent: no escaping the covenant victory of YHWH's people.
16: 'But he would feed you with the finest of the wheat, and with honey from the rock I would satisfy you' — the covenant abundance promised: 'finest wheat' (chelev chittah, the fat/best of wheat) and 'honey from the rock' (dvash misela). These are the Canaan-plenty images: the land flowing with milk and honey (Exod 3:8; Deut 8:8). 'Honey from the rock' appears in Deut 32:13 (Moses' song: 'he made him suck honey out of the rock') — the paradox of sweetness from hardness, abundance from the most barren source. The divine provision transcends even physical impossibility.
